content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
August 11, 1992

Arlyn C. Jorgenson
City Clerk
P.O. Box 391
Redwood City, CA  94064

RE:  Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. A-92-406

Dear Ms. Jorgenson:

     You have asked for advice concerning your duties as a filing
officer under the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1/

QUESTION

     What is a filing officer's duty with respect to notifying an
individual of his/her campaign filing obligations once it becomes
apparent that the individual is making expenditures and soliciting
contributions for election to office?

CONCLUSION

     When a filing officer possesses records, such as a statement
of organization or declaration of candidacy, indicating that a
candidate or committee has an obligation to file a campaign
statement in the filing officer's jurisdiction, and the candidate
or committee has failed to do so, the filing officer has a duty to
notify the candidate or committee of that obligation.  When a
filing officer receives information through newspaper articles,
advertisements, or mailings that a person is receiving
contributions or making expenditures for political purposes, the
filing officer has no duty to notify that person.

     However, in an effort to assist persons who may have filing
obligations but are unaware of the disclosure requirements of the
Act, filing officers are encouraged to contact such persons to
notify them of those requirements.

_________________________

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800  •  P.O. Box 807  •  Sacramento, CA  95812  •  (916) 322-5660

Arlyn C. Jorgenson
Our File No. A-92-406
Page 2


FACTS

You have sent the Commission three articles relating to a
recall effort taking place in Redwood City. A group of citizens
has formed the Redwood Citizens for Recall. As of July 21, 1992,
the Secretary of State's office had not received a Statement of
Organization, Form 410, from Redwood Citizens for Recall, and the
Fair Political Practices Commission has not received Forms 501/502
for any of the candidates appearing in the brochure dated July 7,
1992. On June 30, 1992, you wrote to one of the individuals
involved in the recall effort informing him of his potential
filing obligations.

ANALYSIS

The Act requires individuals who qualify as a committee
pursuant to Section 82013 to file various campaign statements.
(Sections 84101, 84200-84221, 85200, 85201.)

Filing officers are required to notify promptly all persons
and known committees who have failed to file statements. (Section
81010.) In addition, Regulation 18110(a)(1) and (2) provide:

(a) It shall be the duty of a filing officer pursuant
to Section 81010(b) in connection with the filing
of candidate and committee campaign statements to
determine whether required documents have been
filed.

(1) In order to fulfill his or her duty to
determine whether required documents have been
filed, if the filing officer is aware that a
candidate or committee has an obligation to file a
campaign statement and has failed to do so, the
filing officer shall notify the candidate or
committee of the obligation to file a statement.

(2) In determining whether required documents have
been filed, the filing officer shall not be required
to conduct any investigation to determine whether or

Arlyn C. Jorgenson
Our File No. A-92-406
Page 3


not a committee or candidate has an obligation to
file a campaign statement. This subsection is not
intended to prevent the filing officer from
performing such an investigation.

(Emphasis added.)

A filing officer is aware that a candidate or a committee has
an obligation to file disclosure statements when the filing
officer has received official documents establishing the
candidate's or committee's obligation, such as a Statement of
Organization, Form 410, filed by a candidate or committee, or a
declaration of candidacy. A filing officer may also become aware
that a candidate or committee has failed to file required reports
while reviewing campaign statements. For example, while reviewing
a committee's semi-annual campaign statement, it may become
apparent from the activities disclosed that the committee was also
required, and had failed, to file an earlier pre-election
statement.

However, the Act does not require notification by a filing
officer when it appears that an individual has a filing
requirement based on mailings received, or articles appearing in a
newspaper. Whenever it appears that a person may have a filing
requirement, filing officers are encouraged to contact that person
to notify him or her of the campaign disclosure requirements of
the Act.

If you have any questions regarding this letter, please
contact me at 916/322-5662.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Mary Ann Kvasager
Political Reform Consultant

When a filing officer possesses records,
such as a statement
of organization or declaration of candidacy,
indicating that a
candidate or committee has an obligation to file a campaign
statement in the filing officer’s jurisdiction,
and the candidate
or committee has failed to do so, the filing officer has a duty to
notify the candidate or committee of that obligation.
When a
filing officer receives information through newspaper articles,
advertisements,
or mailings that a person is receiving
contributions or making expenditures for political purposes,
the
filing officer has no duty to notify that person.
However,
in an effort to assist persons who may have filing
obligations but are unaware of the disclosure requirements of the
Act, filing officers are encouraged to contact such persons to
notify them of those requirements.

Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.

•
(916) 322-5660


Arlyn C.
Jorgenson
Our File No.
A—92-406
